title: scenario_fire_after_action_review
description: Following a complex structure fire with multiple tactical challenges, the company officer must lead an effective after-action review — identifying what worked, what didn't, and actionable improvements without blame.

timeline.title: Incident Summary
timeline.description: Multi-story residential fire: one rescue, delayed water supply, radio communications breakdown, positive outcome. Crew returned to station two hours ago.

timeline.title: AAR Scheduled
timeline.description: After-action review is scheduled for 30 minutes after return. All four crew members are present.

timeline.title: Communication Issue
timeline.description: One crew member blames another for the radio breakdown during a critical phase.

timeline.title: Positive Recognition
timeline.description: The rescue was executed effectively under pressure — crew members should be recognized.

timeline.title: Systemic Issues
timeline.description: The water supply delay was caused by a mapping error in dispatch — a systemic issue, not an individual error.

timeline.title: Action Items
timeline.description: The AAR must produce specific, assigned, actionable follow-up items.

node-1.title: Opening the AAR
node-1.situation: You are the company officer opening the after-action review. The incident involved a successful rescue, but also a significant water supply delay and radio communication breakdown. Tension is present in the room — one crew member made a sarcastic comment during the return about the radio issue. You need to establish the right tone from the start.
node-1.question: How do you open the AAR?

options.label: A
options.text: Open by stating the purpose clearly: this is a learning review, not a blame session. Acknowledge the successful rescue as a team effort. State that the goal is to identify what worked, what didn't, and what can be improved — for every crew member's future safety. Establish ground rules: speak to actions and systems, not to individuals. Confirm that anything raised stays within the team unless systemic issues need escalation.

options.label: B
options.text: Open by immediately addressing the radio breakdown since that is what the crew is most tense about.

options.label: C
options.text: Open by having each crew member rate the incident from 1–10 and explain their score — this surfaces issues organically.

node-2.title: Blame Emerges
node-2.situation: During discussion of the radio communication breakdown, one crew member directly says to another: 'You were supposed to switch to Channel 3 and you didn't — that's why we couldn't reach command for four minutes.' The accused crew member becomes defensive. The exchange is starting to become personal.
node-2.question: How do you intervene?

options.label: A
options.text: Intervene immediately and calmly. Redirect the discussion: 'Let's describe what happened and when — not who did what.' Ask both crew members to describe the sequence of events factually. Identify whether the channel switch procedure was clearly communicated before entry and whether there is a training or protocol gap. Redirect to the system, not the individual.

options.label: B
options.text: Allow the crew to resolve it themselves — interpersonal accountability is part of team growth.

options.label: C
options.text: Side with the crew member who raised the issue — if the procedure was clear, accountability is appropriate.

node-3.title: Systemic Issue — Dispatch Mapping Error
node-3.situation: The water supply delay is traced to an incorrect hydrant location on the dispatch mapping system — the nearest hydrant shown in dispatch CAD was out of service and not updated. This is a systemic, department-wide safety issue, not an individual crew error. This needs to go beyond this AAR.
node-3.question: How do you handle a systemic issue discovered in an AAR?

options.label: A
options.text: Document the specific dispatch CAD error with exact details — hydrant ID, location, and incorrect status shown. Flag it as a systemic safety finding requiring escalation to the dispatch supervisor and department safety officer. Assign yourself as the responsible party to submit a formal safety report within 24 hours. Acknowledge to the crew that this was not their error and thank them for surfacing it.

options.label: B
options.text: Note it informally and mention it to the battalion chief at the next opportunity — formal reports are for serious injuries, not operational near-misses.

options.label: C
options.text: Leave it out of the formal action items — the crew knows about it and will work around the mapping issue in future.

node-4.title: Scenario Complete
node-4.situation: The AAR was conducted effectively. The tone was set for learning, not blame. Personal conflict was redirected to systems and process. The systemic dispatch mapping error was escalated with a specific owner and deadline. The successful rescue was acknowledged as a team achievement. Action items were documented, assigned, and have clear owners. Well done — an effective after-action review is one of the most powerful tools a company officer has to improve crew safety and performance.
node-4.question: Scenario complete. Well done!
